claim: I have three frontier founders with us—three good-looking guys, actually, and a fourth good-looking guy, Naval.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Vercel into an AI cloud for the world of agents and whatever comes after that.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Boom Supersonic—supersonic aircraft, in his own factory, and jet engines as well.
speaker: Nivi

claim: What principles are they discovering that other founders can learn from?
speaker: Nivi

claim: I can’t remember my exact quote, but I’ve been really pilled with this idea of software factories.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The job of the engineer being something where you just show up to work, you ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was—”how good is person A at shipping output B?” And now what’s happening is, the way I’m judging you as an engineer is, “are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z?” That’s a pretty significant change.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We used to believe—and it used to be somewhat controversial—that there are 10x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now clearly there’s 100x or 1,000x engineers, and the world hasn’t fully adjusted to this.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I used to get flamed on Twitter for saying there are 10x engineers, because it flies in the face of so much equality philosophy that everyone’s equal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the reality is, when you’re operating in idea domains, in intellectual and virtual digital domains, it’s not even 10x—it’s 100x or 1,000x, and it always has been.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The guy who invented JavaScript, the Brendan Eichs of the world.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Not to even mention—if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it could just be not necessarily a better programmer, just one who had better judgment on what to work on in the first place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And now obviously it’s less controversial because of AI leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: What’s controversial is the token leaderboards.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: People are still getting a little confused—”Well, I have a bunch of 100x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Look at all these tokens that I’m paying for.” I’m curious if you guys have seen the same—how do you measure ROI?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s like the old measuring of lines of code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Token consumption and lines of code feel like similarly not direct paradigms.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: My observation has been that Claude or ChatGPT is basically as good as you are in a domain.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’re a really capable developer, these things are really powerful.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’re a junior developer, you’ll find it to be more of a junior developer.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The feedback you give them sporadically seems to be incredibly important—these little updates seem to totally determine the types of performance you get out of them.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There’s a new kind of support I give now—you come to me, you didn’t get good output out of the model, and I tell you what to prompt the model with.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The quality of the reprompting is extremely important.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To be clear, I think this will become less important over time.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: As the models get much smarter, you’ll be able to put in less and get more out.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But at this stage, it really seems to reflect back the judgment that the user brings in.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I’ve kind of resisted learning all the tricks and tips.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Always use plan mode.”

I just ignored all of that.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I assumed the model is going to get better faster than I would figure out how to use it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It would figure out how to use me faster than I would figure out how to use it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I’ve just been completely ham-fisted with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I get frustrated at them and have found myself typing less and less information, doing less and less work as time goes on, because I just assume I can brute-force my way through it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’ll throw Codex, Claude, and Gemini at the same problem over and over and just waste tokens to save time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: No matter how expensive these models might seem, they’re still way cheaper than a human.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Don’t look at the tokens either as inputs or outputs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Just look at your time, and look at the final output.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Even if they’re writing low-quality code—which I know in many cases they are—when the time comes and I want to ship to production, I’ll just throw more tokens at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
“Go through, look at it, rewrite it.”

They’re just going to get better every generation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as we have verifiable domains and solved problems, they’re going to resolve those problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now in the unsolved problems domain—maybe you’re Terence Tao, at the cutting edge of creativity—you need to be working very collaboratively and carefully and closely with the model.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But I’m not at that level in software engineering.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Guillermo, you’re probably the most extreme software engineer on the team.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: How are you finding these models at the edge of their capability?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’s one thing that’s happened recently that resonates strongly with what you’re saying.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It used to be that you’d give a prompt to the model and it kind of does a classic next-token prediction thing and runs away with your idea.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Models now have been doing this intuitive planning mode—without you even having to ask them to plan—where it comes back to you and says, “Look, what you’re asking me for, there are these three routes we can take.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Here’s the set of trade-offs.”

That’s the moment where people on X do the whole thing—”Now we have a PhD-level engineer model.”

The models at some point graduated.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now they’re principal engineers, because they come back to you with a set of trade-offs.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And obviously, sometimes they bullshit, which is hilarious—it tells you “this is going to take three weeks” and “this many tokens.” It makes really bad predictions.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But I respect the models a lot more as a peer that I’m going back and forth intellectually with.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you’re a really proficient engineer or architect, you’re still extracting more juice.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: So the question Max was positing—”if you’re junior, do you get junior back?” Clearly not, because a junior gets more advanced knowledge in code than they would have been able to write by themselves.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But doesn’t an experienced architect get 10x where a junior engineer gets 2x?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’m seeing this now with some of our junior software engineers on the team—what’s the next step in their career progression?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going from writing implementation for a feature to picking technologies.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Choosing between Postgres versus some other database.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Picking between ZeroMQ versus some other queuing system.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The models can suggest them, but that’s the thing—you’ll see it and you’ll go, “No, no, I want to use this other thing.” That’s the type of little feedback that really matters and the types of output you seem to get at this point.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That said—you can ask the models “which one should I use and why,” and they know everything.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’ll give you a really good trade-offs matrix.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’d say, “Hey, go put this super-high-cardinality telemetry data into Postgres.” And it goes, “No, no, bro.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We don’t put that kind of data into Postgres.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You should consider ClickHouse or Athena or whatever.” That’s happened to me a lot.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing I’m still struggling with is—clearly the human is still completing the model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The human is the one starting to get the instructions back: “Go get me this API key, because it’s something only you can do.” Or “Get me this amount of capital for my next set of investments.” You just watch.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Pretty soon every good SaaS company or hosting provider will have a CLI and API interface the models can use directly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as it’s text-based, Unix-based—the agent can hack its own API.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And the money part—you insert crypto tokens, put in Bitcoin, put in whatever, and the model goes and pays for whatever it needs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing I’m now thinking through is—is pure software dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software engineering an obsolete thing?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We had to learn code to communicate with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now the models speak English—fuzzy, sloppy English, like a human—and they understand things.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You had to build hardware, and it was hard to build a software company alongside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Patrick Collison says, “Software is art, and it’s hard to hire artists.”

Now, as a hardware founder—great, you can have really good software developed fairly quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re creating models, maybe that’s the new software engineering—training, tweaking, post-training, fine-tuning.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But classic software engineering—is that dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software something you can organize a company and a team around, and try to get some leverage?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Did you guys see—there was an article on X by Mitchell Hashimoto called “The Building Block Economy”?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: His argument is that the most useful thing for agents to have now is really powerful reusable building blocks.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To Max’s example, you wouldn’t expect your clanker to reinvent a queue infrastructure system every time it needs to send an email.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
It needs to bring in the right building block, right-sized for the task—”Okay, for this one it’s BullMQ.”

I challenge the notion that I’d want the agent to reinvent the entire universe from first principles in a way that’s incompatible with the rest of society and civilization.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s almost like reinventing highways, laws, policies—just for you.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Even if there’s potential for extra optimization, there’s still cooperation-at-large-scale value of saying “we’re both depending on Postgres 13.2.”

The category of infrastructure software and building blocks these agents are going to use is—obviously in bias, this is what we’re building—extremely valuable.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I don’t see the agent reinventing all of that any time soon.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another metaphor I’ve been using: anything that’s already been created that the models can reuse is like a token cache.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You don’t want to churn through a trillion tokens to reproduce what’s already existing.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s always a starting point the model can fork off from.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s going to change things quite profoundly.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: So these are like libraries and dependencies, but for models.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Through all of being a teenager and in my twenties, I’d get sucked into it and code for like twenty hours.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I haven’t written a single line of code in quite a while now.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But also—since December, I’ve built a huge amount of software that I now use every day.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: And I just can’t imagine going back to actually writing code by hand.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I have a hard time seeing that as part of the future.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Anyone who understands what an API is, how data flows, inputs and outputs, performance—because you have to orient the model around “this is the level of expectation I have out of this operation.” That has always been infinitely more useful than writing code.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: A really proficient engineering leader has been quote-unquote vibe coding through people on Slack or one-on-ones—you’re transmitting your will, your intent, your experience, and letting others run with it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I went from not having written code in twenty years to coding all the time now—through agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It turns out that just understanding the basic principles of software engineering and algorithms gets you a long way.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The reason I stopped coding was that I didn’t have time to figure out the latest language, the latest architecture, the infrastructure pieces to plug into.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing that really changed is—it used to be you could build a lot, a lot would go straightforward, but then you’d hit some random thing and you could spend an indefinite period of time debugging some narrow thing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now, with agents, you just don’t get stuck anymore.
speaker: Max Hodak
